initial_program:
% 1. Grid dimensions
n_rows(N) :- N = #count{R : input(R, _, _)}.
n_cols(N) :- N = #count{C : input(_, C, _)}.
row(0..N-1) :- N = #count{R : input(R, _, _)}.
col(0..N-1) :- N = #count{C : input(_, C, _)}.

% 2. Output grid is 3x3 (the problem guarantees a 33 output)
output_cell(R, C) :- row(R), col(C).   % rows and columns are 0..2

% 3. Each output cell must get exactly one colour
1 { output(R, C, Color) : color(Color) } 1 :- output_cell(R, C).

% 4. Helper aggregates
% digit counts in the input
digitCount(D) :- #count{R,C : input(R,C,D)}.

% row counts (nonzero cells per row)
rowCount(R) :- #count{C : input(R,C,Color)}.

% find the three rows with the highest rowCount.
% maxRowCount is the highest row count.
maxRowCount(N) :- #max{R : rowCount(R)} = N.

% rows with that maximum count
rowMax(R) :- rowCount(R) = maxRowCount(N).

% second best rows: rows whose count is either maxRowCount or maxRowCount-1,
% but only keep up to three rows.  (The three examples all have exactly three
% rows with count >=2, so this will pick rows 1,2,3 for the examples.)
rowSel(R,Idx) :-
    rowCount(R) >= maxRowCount(N)-1,
    #count{R : rowCount(R) >= maxRowCount(N)-1} = 3,
    #min{R : rowCount(R) >= maxRowCount(N)-1} = R,
    #count{R : rowCount(R) >= maxRowCount(N)-1, R <= 9} = 3,
    Idx = 0..2.

% For each of the three selected rows (call them r0,r1,r2) compute the
% dominant colour  the colour with the highest frequency in that row.
dominant(I, D) :-
    rowSel(i,i) = true,               % i is the index of the selected row
    #count{C : input(i+1, C, D)} = #max{C' : #count{R : #count{C' : input(R, C', D)}}.

% first column where the dominant colour appears in that row
firstCol(I, C) :-
    dominant(i,D),
    input(i+1, C, D),
    #min{C' : input(i+1, C', D)} = C.

% extra colour: the most frequent digit that still has unused copies
% (global most frequent digit that has not been used up yet)
extra(I, D) :-
    dominant(i,D2),
    digitCount(D) = #max{K : digitCount(K)},
    #count{R,C : output(R,C,D)} = 0,               % not yet used
    #count{R,C : output(R,C,K)} < digitCount(K) for all K.   % still room for at least one copy

% Map each output cell (i,j) to an input cell.
output(R, C, Color) :-
    % the three output rows correspond to the three selected input rows
    i = R,                                    % output row i corresponds to selected row i
    j = C,                                    % column j

    % start column for this row (shift left for odd rows)
    startCol = 0,
    rowSel(i,i) = true,                       % i is selected
    dominant(i,Dstart),
    firstCol(i,SC),
    (i mod 2 = 0) -> startCol = SC,           % even rows: no shift
    (i mod 2 = 1) -> startCol = SC-1,         % odd rows: shift left by one

    colIdx = startCol + j,
    colIdx < n_cols(N),
    (input(i+1, colIdx, Color) = 1)           % copy the colour from the mapped cell
    % but if the mapped cell is zero, we may use the extra colour instead
    (input(i+1, colIdx, Color) = 0) -> 
        extra(i,Color).

% any remaining cells (should not happen in the given examples) are zero
output(R, C, 0) :-
    not(output(R, C, D)) :- !output(R, C, D2).   % ensure a colour is assigned

% 5. Global constraints  each colour may be used at most as often as it appears in the input
forall(D : color(0..9),
    #count{R,C : output(R,C,D)} <= digitCount(D).

% 6. Safety checks  every variable used in a positive rule must appear positively
:- forall(D : color(0..9), #count{R : output(R, _, D)} > 0  :- output(R, _, D).

forall(D : color(0..9),
    #count{R,C : output(R,C,D)} <= digitCount(D).

% 6. Safety checks  every variable used in a positive rule must appear positively

final_program:
% 1. Grid dimensions
n_rows(N) :- N = #count{R : input(R, _, _)}.
n_cols(N) :- N = #count{C : input(_, C, _)}.
row(0..N-1) :- N = #count{R : input(R, _, _)}.
col(0..N-1) :- N = #count{C : input(_, C, _)}.

% 2. Output grid is 3x3 (the problem guarantees a 33 output)
output_cell(R, C) :- row(R), col(C).   % rows and columns are 0..2

% 3. Each output cell must get exactly one colour
1 { output(R, C, Color) : color(Color) } 1 :- output_cell(R, C).

% 4. Helper aggregates
% digit counts in the input
digitCount(D) :- #count{R,C : input(R,C,D)}.

% row counts (nonzero cells per row)
rowCount(R) :- #count{C : input(R,C,Color)}.

% find the three rows with the highest rowCount.
% maxRowCount is the highest row count.
maxRowCount(N) :- #max{R : rowCount(R)} = N.

% rows with that maximum count
rowMax(R) :- rowCount(R) = maxRowCount(N).

% second best rows: rows whose count is either maxRowCount or maxRowCount-1,
% but only keep up to three rows.  (The three examples all have exactly three
% rows with count >=2, so this will pick rows 1,2,3 for the examples.)
rowSel(R,Idx) :-
    rowCount(R) >= maxRowCount(N)-1,
    #count{R : rowCount(R) >= maxRowCount(N)-1} = 3,
    #min{R : rowCount(R) >= maxRowCount(N)-1} = R,
    #count{R : rowCount(R) >= maxRowCount(N)-1, R <= 9} = 3,
    Idx = 0..2.

% For each of the three selected rows (call them r0,r1,r2) compute the
% dominant colour  the colour with the highest frequency in that row.
dominant(I, D) :-
    rowSel(i,i) = true,               % i is the index of the selected row
    #count{C : input(i+1, C, D)} = #max{C' : #count{R : #count{C' : input(R, C', D)}}.

% first column where the dominant colour appears in that row
firstCol(I, C) :-
    dominant(i,D),
    input(i+1, C, D),
    #min{C' : input(i+1, C', D)} = C.

% extra colour: the most frequent digit that still has unused copies
% (global most frequent digit that has not been used up yet)
extra(I, D) :-
    dominant(i,D2),
    digitCount(D) = #max{K : digitCount(K)},
    #count{R,C : output(R,C,D)} = 0,               % not yet used
    #count{R,C : output(R,C,K)} < digitCount(K) for all K.   % still room for at least one copy

% Map each output cell (i,j) to an input cell.
output(R, C, Color) :-
    % the three output rows correspond to the three selected input rows
    i = R,                                    % output row i corresponds to selected row i
    j = C,                                    % column j

    % start column for this row (shift left for odd rows)
    startCol = 0,
    rowSel(i,i) = true,                       % i is selected
    dominant(i,Dstart),
    firstCol(i,SC),
    (i mod 2 = 0) -> startCol = SC,           % even rows: no shift
    (i mod 2 = 1) -> startCol = SC-1,         % odd rows: shift left by one

    colIdx = startCol + j,
    colIdx < n_cols(N),
    (input(i+1, colIdx, Color) = 1)           % copy the colour from the mapped cell
    % but if the mapped cell is zero, we may use the extra colour instead
    (input(i+1, colIdx, Color) = 0) -> 
        extra(i,Color).

% any remaining cells (should not happen in the given examples) are zero
output(R, C, 0) :-
    not(output(R, C, D)) :- !output(R, C, D2).   % ensure a colour is assigned

% 5. Global constraints  each colour may be used at most as often as it appears in the input
forall(D : color(0..9),
    #count{R,C : output(R,C,D)} <= digitCount(D).

% 6. Safety checks  every variable used in a positive rule must appear positively
:- forall(D : color(0..9), #count{R : output(R, _, D)} > 0  :- output(R, _, D).
